questions.question: Which of the following signs is part of the classic triad of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Areflexia is an essential component of the classic triad of Guillain-Barré syndrome, along with ascending paralysis and albuminocytological dissociation. The other options correspond to upper motor neuron signs, not this demyelinating polyneuropathy.

questions.question: In Guillain-Barré syndrome, albuminocytological dissociation in the CSF is characterized by:
questions.explanation: Albuminocytological dissociation consists of elevated proteins in the cerebrospinal fluid without a significant increase in cells (pleocytosis). It is a classic finding of Guillain-Barré syndrome, although it may be absent in the initial stages.

questions.question: What is the main characteristic of the paralysis pattern in Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Symmetric ascending paralysis is a hallmark of Guillain-Barré syndrome: it begins in the lower limbs and progresses to the upper limbs and trunk. Unlike descending paralysis (botulism) or hemiparesis (CVA), here the pattern is ascending and symmetrical.

questions.question: Which of the following CSF findings supports the diagnosis of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Albuminocytological dissociation (elevated proteins with normal cell count) is the typical finding. Neutrophilic pleocytosis suggests bacterial meningitis; low glucorrhachia is seen in tuberculous or fungal meningitis; Elevated pressure indicates intracranial hypertension.

questions.question: In the classic triad of Guillain-Barré syndrome, areflexia is mainly due to:
questions.explanation: Areflexia in Guillain-Barré is a consequence of demyelination of peripheral nerves, which interrupts nerve conduction in the reflex arc. The upper motor neuron (damaged in ALS) produces hyperreflexia; the anterior horn (poliomyelitis) gives areflexia but with atrophy; Neuromuscular blockade (myasthenia) does not cause areflexia.

questions.question: Which of the following is a first-line treatment for Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) is one of the first-line treatments along with plasmapheresis. Corticosteroids have not shown benefit and may even worsen the prognosis. Interferon beta is used in multiple sclerosis; Azathioprine is a maintenance immunosuppressant, not an acute phase immunosuppressant.

questions.question: What is the most common variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (AIDP) is the most common variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome, accounting for approximately 90% of cases in Europe and North America.

questions.question: What electrophysiological finding characterizes the AIDP variant?
options: Driving lock and reduced driving speed
Decreased amplitude of the motor action potential
Presence of F waves absent only in sensory nerves
questions.explanation: Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy is characterized by conduction block, reduced nerve conduction velocity, and increased distal latency, typical findings of demyelination.

questions.question: Which antibody is most frequently associated with the AIDP variant?
questions.explanation: Unlike axonal variants, acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy does not have a consistent association with specific antiganglioside antibodies; anti-GM1 or anti-GD1a antibodies are more typical of axonal forms.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for the AIDP variant?
questions.explanation: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) is the first-line treatment for acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, along with plasmapheresis; Corticosteroids have not shown benefit and are not recommended.

questions.question: Which antecedent infection is most related to the AIDP variant?
questions.explanation: Although Campylobacter jejuni is the most common trigger of Guillain-Barré syndrome in general, the acute demyelinating variant (AIDP) is more frequently associated with cytomegalovirus infection, while C. jejuni is more associated with axonal forms.

questions.question: What is the most typical initial clinical sign of the AIDP variant?
questions.explanation: Progressive, symmetrical ascending muscle weakness beginning in the lower extremities is the most characteristic initial clinical sign of acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, often accompanied by paresthesias.

questions.question: What infection is the most common antecedent in Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Campylobacter jejuni is the most common infectious agent associated with Guillain-Barré syndrome, triggering an autoimmune response against peripheral nerve gangliosides.

questions.question: Which of the following agents is associated with the acute motor axonal variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Campylobacter jejuni is frequently associated with the acute motor axonal variant (AMAN) of Guillain-Barré syndrome, due to molecular mimicry with gangliosides such as GM1.

questions.question: What infection prior to Guillain-Barré syndrome is associated with bilateral facial paralysis and sensory impairment?
questions.explanation: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) is associated with Guillain-Barré disease that often includes bilateral facial paralysis and sensory impairment, frequently in young patients.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism do Campylobacter jejuni infections and Guillain-Barré syndrome share?
questions.explanation: Molecular mimicry between Campylobacter jejuni lipopolysaccharides and myelin gangliosides triggers an autoimmune response that damages peripheral nerves.

questions.question: Which of these infectious agents is associated with a more severe and rapidly progressive form of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Campylobacter jejuni is usually associated with more severe and rapidly progressive forms, such as the axonal variant, with a worse functional prognosis and a greater need for mechanical ventilation.

questions.question: What Mycoplasma pneumoniae infection is Guillain-Barré syndrome characterized by?
questions.explanation: Mycoplasma pneumoniae is associated with the classic picture of symmetric ascending paralysis in Guillain-Barré syndrome, with weakness progressing from the lower extremities.

questions.question: What is the characteristic CSF finding of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: In Guillain-Barré syndrome, the CSF typically shows elevation of proteins without significant increase in cells (albuminocytological dissociation).

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism explains hyperproteinorrachia in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: Demyelinating inflammation of the nerve roots increases the permeability of the blood-brain barrier, allowing the passage of proteins into the CSF.

questions.question: What is the most important differential diagnosis for CSF with lymphocytic pleocytosis?
questions.explanation: Lymphocytic pleocytosis is characteristic of viral meningitis, not Guillain-Barré, which presents albuminocytological dissociation.

questions.question: What protein value in CSF is considered suggestive of Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: In Guillain-Barré, proteins usually exceed 45 mg/dL, often up to 100-200 mg/dL, without significant pleocytosis (<10 cells/μL).

questions.question: At what point in the Guillain-Barré course does hyperproteinorrachia usually appear?
options: From the first day of symptoms
questions.explanation: Elevation of CSF protein may take up to 7-10 days to manifest; At the beginning it may be normal, which does not rule out the diagnosis.

questions.question: What finding in the CSF rules out the diagnosis of Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: A significant pleocytosis (>10-50 cells/μL) is atypical for Guillain-Barré and requires ruling out other infectious or inflammatory causes.

questions.question: What electromyographic finding characterizes demyelinating Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: In the demyelinating variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome, electromyography shows multifocal conduction block due to segmental demyelination of peripheral nerves.

questions.question: What electrophysiological abnormality is associated with proximal axonal injury in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: F waves reflect proximal nerve conduction; Its absence indicates blockage or damage to the proximal nerve root, a common finding in Guillain-Barré syndrome.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of conduction block in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: Segmental demyelination interrupts Saltatory conduction, producing blockage of nerve impulse transmission, characteristic of the acute demyelinating variant.

questions.question: What electrophysiological study confirms proximal involvement in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: The F wave evaluates the proximal conduction of the motor nerve; Its prolongation or absence indicates damage to the root or proximal segment, a typical alteration in Guillain-Barré.

questions.question: What electromyographic pattern suggests axonal variant of Guillain-Barré?
options: F waves absent at normal velocities
questions.explanation: In the axonal variant, conduction velocities are usually normal or slightly reduced, but F waves are absent due to proximal axonal damage without significant demyelination.

questions.question: What electrophysiological finding rules out Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: The absence of conduction block and normal F waves in multiple nerves makes the diagnosis of Guillain-Barré, which typically shows proximal abnormalities and demyelination, unlikely.

questions.question: Which anti-ganglioside antibody is typically associated with the acute motor axonal variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Anti-GM1 antibodies are associated with the acute motor axonal variant (AMAN) of Guillain-Barré syndrome, mediating direct damage to motor axons.

questions.question: Which anti-ganglioside antibody is characteristic of Miller Fisher syndrome?
questions.explanation: Anti-GQ1b antibodies are found in more than 90% of cases of Miller Fisher syndrome, a variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome with ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexia.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of anti-GM1 antibodies in axonal Guillain-Barré syndrome?
options: Direct damage to the motor axon
questions.explanation: Anti-GM1 antibodies bind to GM1 gangliosides on the axonal membrane, activating complement and causing direct damage to the motor axon without primary demyelination.

questions.question: What ganglioside is the main target of antibodies in Miller Fisher syndrome?
questions.explanation: The GQ1b ganglioside is abundantly expressed in the oculomotor nerves, explaining the ophthalmoplegia characteristic of Miller Fisher syndrome when attacked by anti-GQ1b antibodies.

questions.question: Which of the following clinical syndromes is most frequently associated with anti-GQ1b antibodies?
questions.explanation: Anti-GQ1b antibodies are highly specific for Miller Fisher syndrome, characterized by the triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia and areflexia, differentiating it from other neuropathies.

questions.question: What first-line treatment is recommended for Guillain-Barré syndrome associated with anti-GM1 antibodies?
questions.explanation: Both plasmapheresis and intravenous immunoglobulin are first-line treatments in Guillain-Barré syndrome, including the axonal variant with anti-GM1 antibodies, as they reduce progression and accelerate recovery.

questions.question: What is the classic triad of the Miller Fisher variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: The characteristic triad of the Miller Fisher variant is ophthalmoplegia, ataxia and areflexia, which distinguishes it from other variants of Guillain-Barré syndrome.

questions.question: Which antibody is typically associated with the Miller Fisher variant?
questions.explanation: Anti-GQ1b antibody is found in the majority of patients with Miller Fisher variant, targeting the gangliosides of the oculomotor nerve and dorsal root.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for the Miller Fisher variant?
questions.explanation: Intravenous immunoglobulin is the treatment of choice for the Miller Fisher variant, showing effectiveness in reducing the duration of symptoms.

questions.question: Which cranial nerve is most commonly affected in Miller Fisher ophthalmoplegia?
questions.explanation: Ophthalmoplegia in Miller Fisher predominantly affects the oculomotor nerve (III), causing limitation of vertical and horizontal gaze.

questions.question: What complementary study is diagnostic in the Miller Fisher variant?
questions.explanation: Cerebrospinal fluid analysis shows albuminocytological dissociation (elevated proteins with normal cells), typical of Guillain-Barré syndrome and its variants.

questions.question: What is the typical prognosis for the Miller Fisher variant?
questions.explanation: The Miller Fisher variant has a favorable prognosis, with spontaneous recovery or treatment in weeks to months, without significant motor sequelae.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment in severe Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Intravenous immunoglobulin is the treatment of choice in severe Guillain-Barré syndrome, along with plasmapheresis, due to its effectiveness in reducing the duration of the disease and improving the prognosis.

questions.question: What therapeutic alternative is equally effective as immunoglobulin in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: Plasmapheresis has demonstrated comparable efficacy to intravenous immunoglobulin in the treatment of Guillain-Barré syndrome, both being first-line options in severe cases.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of intravenous immunoglobulin in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: Intravenous immunoglobulin acts by modulating the immune response, neutralizing autoantibodies and reducing inflammation in peripheral nerves, which improves clinical outcome.

questions.question: At what stage of Guillain-Barré is it recommended to start treatment with immunoglobulin?
questions.explanation: Treatment with intravenous immunoglobulin should be initiated within the first two weeks from the onset of symptoms to maximize its effectiveness and reduce the progression of weakness.

questions.question: Which adverse effect is most characteristic of intravenous immunoglobulin in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: Headache and aseptic meningitis are known adverse effects of intravenous immunoglobulin, due to the meningeal inflammatory reaction, although they are usually self-limiting.

questions.question: What absolute contraindication prevents the use of plasmapheresis in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: Hemodynamic instability is an absolute contraindication for plasmapheresis, since the procedure can cause sudden changes in blood pressure and worsen the patient's condition.

questions.question: In which situation of Guillain-Barré syndrome is plasmapheresis indicated?
questions.explanation: Plasmapheresis is indicated in Guillain-Barré syndrome when there is rapid progression or respiratory failure, as it can reduce the duration of mechanical ventilation and accelerate recovery.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Guillain-Barré syndrome is an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy mediated by autoantibodies against peripheral myelin gangliosides.

questions.question: Which cerebrospinal fluid finding is typical of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Albuminocytological dissociation (elevated proteins with normal cell count) is characteristic of Guillain-Barré syndrome, reflecting inflammation of the nerve roots without direct infection.

questions.question: What electrophysiological study supports the diagnosis of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: In Guillain-Barré syndrome, nerve conduction velocities are usually slow or show conduction block, evidencing segmental demyelination of peripheral nerves.

questions.question: What immunomodulatory treatment is an alternative to plasmapheresis in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: Intravenous immunoglobulin is as effective as plasmapheresis in Guillain-Barré syndrome and is preferred due to its lower technical complexity and safety profile.

questions.question: What indication contraindicates the use of plasmapheresis in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: Severe hemodynamic instability contraindicates plasmapheresis due to the risk of hypotension and complications during the plasma exchange procedure.

questions.question: What is the forced vital capacity value that indicates admission to the ICU in Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: In Guillain-Barré syndrome, a forced vital capacity of less than 20 mL/kg is a criterion for admission to the intensive care unit due to the risk of respiratory failure.

questions.question: What respiratory parameter is used as a criterion for admission to the ICU in Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Forced vital capacity is the standard parameter to evaluate respiratory risk in Guillain-Barré syndrome, with a threshold of 20 mL/kg for admission to the ICU.

questions.question: What complication justifies admission to the ICU with forced vital capacity less than 20 mL/kg in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: Forced vital capacity less than 20 mL/kg indicates severe weakness of the respiratory muscles, with a high risk of respiratory failure that requires monitoring and support in the ICU.

questions.question: What forced vital capacity value contraindicates a stay on the hospital ward in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: A forced vital capacity of less than 20 mL/kg is an absolute criterion for admission to the ICU, which is why it contraindicates a stay in a conventional hospitalization ward.

questions.question: What is the main cause of mortality in Guillain-Barré syndrome that is prevented by admission to the ICU?
questions.explanation: Respiratory failure due to weakness of the respiratory muscles is the main cause of death in Guillain-Barré syndrome, and admission to the ICU with monitoring of forced vital capacity allows it to be anticipated.

questions.question: What intervention should be considered if forced vital capacity falls below 20 mL/kg in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: When the forced vital capacity is less than 20 mL/kg, respiratory weakness is severe and usually requires orotracheal intubation and invasive mechanical ventilation to ensure oxygenation.

questions.question: What percentage of patients with Guillain-Barré syndrome present permanent sequelae?
questions.explanation: Approximately 20% of patients with Guillain-Barré syndrome develop permanent neurological sequelae, indicating incomplete recovery.

questions.question: What is the mortality rate associated with Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Mortality in Guillain-Barré syndrome is around 5%, generally due to complications such as respiratory failure or infections.

questions.question: What is the most common prognosis in patients with Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Most patients with Guillain-Barré syndrome make a complete or near-complete recovery, although it may take months.

questions.question: What factor is associated with a worse prognosis in Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: The need for mechanical ventilation indicates severe involvement of the respiratory nerves and is associated with a worse prognosis and greater risk of sequelae.

questions.question: Which of the following complications contributes to the 5% mortality in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: Respiratory failure due to paralysis of the respiratory muscles is the main cause of death in Guillain-Barré syndrome.

questions.question: What is the typical time for complete recovery in most patients with Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: Complete recovery usually occurs in weeks to months, although some patients may have residual fatigue or weakness for longer.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Intravenous immunoglobulin is the first-line treatment in Guillain-Barré syndrome. Corticosteroids are not indicated and may worsen the prognosis.

questions.question: What effect do corticosteroids have on Guillain-Barré syndrome?
options: They show no benefit and get worse
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids have not shown benefit in Guillain-Barré syndrome and can worsen the clinical course, so they are contraindicated.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an accepted treatment in Guillain-Barré?
questions.explanation: Systemic corticosteroids are contraindicated in Guillain-Barré syndrome because they do not provide benefit and can worsen the condition, unlike immunoglobulin and plasmapheresis.

questions.question: Why are corticosteroids contraindicated in Guillain-Barré syndrome?
options: They do not benefit and can worsen
They increase the risk of infection
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids are contraindicated because clinical studies do not show a benefit in recovery and, in some cases, they are associated with a worse outcome in Guillain-Barré syndrome.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Guillain-Barré syndrome is an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy mediated by autoantibodies against peripheral myelin gangliosides.

questions.question: What antibodies are most frequently associated with Guillain-Barré syndrome?
questions.explanation: Anti-GM1 (anti-ganglioside) antibodies are detected in a significant proportion of patients with Guillain-Barré syndrome, especially in the acute motor axonal variant.
